title: A Satake Isomorphism $mod.p$
speaker: Guy Henniart
abstract: Let F be a locally compact non-Archimedean field, p its residue characteristic and G a connected reductive algebraic group over F . The classical Satake isomorphism describes the Hecke algebra (over the field of complex numbers) of double classes in G with respect to a special maximal compact subgroup K of G .

proof_stages.label: Edwards–Anderson ground-state pairs
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the Edwards–Anderson spin-glass Hamiltonian, passes from finite-volume zero-temperature minimizers to infinite-volume finite-flip stability, and formulates the basic question of uniqueness of ground-state pairs.

proof_stages.label: Observable ground states and metastates
proof_stages.summary: Observable ground-state pairs are defined as limits obtained with coupling-independent boundary conditions, and metastates are built as subsequential limits of joint coupling/ground-state distributions so that two sampled states can be compared.

proof_stages.label: Full-plane interface constraints
proof_stages.summary: For two full-plane ground states the interface is defined, loops are ruled out by the opposite loop-energy calculation, branching is ruled out by a Burton–Keane translation-invariance argument, and the Newman–Stein theorem is stated: a metastate-sampled interface is either empty or a single positive-density path.

proof_stages.label: Half-plane uniqueness theorem and reduction
proof_stages.summary: The half-plane boxes and boundary conditions are fixed, the theorem that the limiting half-plane metastate is supported on one ground-state pair is stated, and the proof is reduced to showing that two metastate-sampled half-plane ground states have empty interface.

proof_stages.label: Vertical averaging to a full-plane measure
proof_stages.summary: The half-plane joint measure is translated downward and averaged to produce a full-plane translation-invariant measure to which the full-plane Newman–Stein interface theorem applies, setting up a contradiction if many domain walls survive far from the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Tethered domain walls at the boundary
proof_stages.summary: Tethered domain walls crossing the x-axis are introduced, shown to cross once and be disjoint, and then horizontal ergodicity plus local modification of couplings gives positive density of such walls at height zero whenever a half-plane interface exists.

proof_stages.label: Non-thinning and contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Counting tethered walls through intervals at height k shows their density cannot decrease with height, so the averaged full-plane measure sees at least two domain walls with positive probability, contradicting the full-plane theorem and proving half-plane uniqueness.

examples.name: One-dimensional spin-glass chain
examples.note: A signed 1D chain is used to show concretely how all bonds can be satisfied and why the ground-state pair is unique in one dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through a 1D chain by assigning spins to satisfy signed bonds and checking that flipping a finite block has positive boundary energy, yielding uniqueness.

examples.name: Burton–Keane percolation argument
examples.note: The no-multiple-infinite-clusters argument from percolation is invoked as the source of the counting idea that rules out branching points in translation-invariant interfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: The Burton–Keane argument is not merely cited; the speaker adapts it by bounding branching points by boundary size and contrasting this with a volume-order expectation.
